comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the concept of autoresearch using LLMs to automate hyperparameter tuning and bug fixing in machine learning projects. While some commenters appreciate the practical benefits and iterative feedback loop, others critique it as mostly incremental optimization rather than true innovation.

comments.author: n_bhavikatti
comments.text: The temperature clamp fix and "Optuna++" actions by the agents (the cause of basically all improvement to eCLIP) indicate they are good at finding bugs and hyper-parameter tuning. But when it comes to anything beyond that, such as novel architectural shifts, agents aren't good enough. With no clear path forward they tend to randomly change things, which is a poor approach. Agents: Optimization >> innovation
comments.analysis: This comment accurately distinguishes between optimization capabilities and innovation limitations of autoresearch agents, reflecting a nuanced understanding of the technology's current scope.

comments.author: carlsborg
comments.text:
> “ The agent acted like a hyperparameter optimization algorithm with some basic reasoning baked in.”

Good lens.

The crux of the auto research repo is basically one file - program.md which is a system prompt that can be summarized as “do this in a loop: improve train.py, run the training, run evals, record result. Favor simplicity”. The other files are an arbitrary ML model that is being trained.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a clear and accurate summary of the autoresearch implementation, helping clarify the mechanism behind the project.

comments.author: _pdp_
comments.text:
Take some working code. Ask an LLM to fix bugs. Measure performance and test coverage. Feed the results back into the LLM. Repeat.

This has been the standard approach for more complex LLM deployments for a while now in our shop.

Using different models across iterations is also something I've found useful in my own experiments. It's like getting a fresh pair of eyes.
comments.analysis: This comment describes a practical iterative approach consistent with known LLM deployment strategies, reinforcing the discussion's technical validity.

comments.author: lucasay
comments.text: This feels less like automated research and more like structured trial and error with a decent feedback loop. Still useful, but I think the real bottleneck is how good your eval metric is. If that’s weak, the whole loop just optimizes for the wrong thing faster.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the importance of evaluation metrics in the autoresearch process, a critical factor often overlooked.

comments.author: datsci_est_2015
comments.text:
I often use LLMs to explore prior art and maybe find some alternative ways of thinking of problems. About 90% of what it tells me is useless or inapplicable to my domain due to a technicality it could not have known, but the other 10% is nice and has helped me learn some great new things.

I can’t imagine letting an agent try everything that the LLM chatbot had recommended ($$$). Often coming up in recommendations are very poorly maintained / niche libraries that have quite a lot of content written about them but what I can only imagine is very limited use in real production environments.

On the other hand, we have domain expert “consultants” in our leadership’s ears making equally absurd recommendations that we constantly have to disprove. Maybe an agent can occupy those consultants and let us do our work in peace.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic user perspective on LLM utility, balancing skepticism with acknowledgment of occasional helpful insights.

comments.author: dvt
comments.text:
Ok, so looking at the commit log[1], I was mostly interested in seeing what the "moonshot ideas" implementations looked like, but basically everything is just hyperparameter tuning. Which is nice, but likely not worth the $$$ spent on the tokens. Am I missing something here?

[1] https://github.com/ykumards/eCLIP/commits/main/autoresearch
comments.analysis: This comment critically questions the cost-benefit ratio of the approach, reflecting a common concern about the value of incremental improvements versus resource expenditure.

comments.author: lamroger
comments.text:
Awesome breakdown! It really feels like a hyper-hyper parameter search + bug fixer.

I started looking at Kaggle again and autoresearch seems to converge to many of the solution vibes there.

Wild ensembles, squeezing a bit of loss out. More engineering than research IMO
comments.analysis: This comment emphasizes the engineering-heavy nature of the approach, aligning with others who see it as optimization rather than groundbreaking research.

comments.author: pikachu0625
comments.text: It's better to outsource optimization phases. Our idea should be for constraint, assumptions etc. for breakthrough. Boyd often argues that once you can express a problem in a standard mathematical form, the implementation becomes a commodity that software can handle automatically.
comments.analysis: This comment suggests a strategic perspective on focusing human effort on problem formulation rather than optimization, which is a valuable insight.

comments.author: SebastianSosa
comments.text:
autoresearch is a trivial research idea
"ablate through experiments with knowledge over previous experiments"
comments.analysis: This comment dismisses the novelty of the idea but correctly summarizes the core concept, though the tone is somewhat dismissive.

comments.author: saidnooneever
comments.text:
pretty cool experiment, i thought about someone maybe doing this and am happy you did it in this way. nice writeup too. this made me giggle a bit: "At one point it got tired of waiting for training to finish and just ended the conversation. I wouldn’t give it full autonomy just yet :)"

thanks for sharing your results and the road to them!
comments.analysis: This comment expresses positive sentiment and appreciation but adds limited technical insight.
